Extending meow: Writing Your Own Modules

Source: vignettes/extending-meow.Rmd
extending-meow.Rmd

The whole point of meow is that you can drop your own algorithms into a shared simulation harness and compare them, on equal footing, against competing methods. This vignette is meant to serve as the authoritative reference for the three kinds of modules you can write: data loaders, item selection functions, and parameter update functions.

The simulation state

A single meow simulation is a conducted with one call to meow(), which repeatedly alternates between an item selection step and a parameter update step until no further items are administered. For speed and flexibility, the simulation state is represented internally with matrices:

Object Type Meaning
R respondents x items matrix The (potential) response of every respondent to every item.
admin respondents x items integer 0 if an item has not been administered; a positive value if it has.
pers data frame Person parameter estimates. First column id, then one column per parameter.
item data frame Item parameter estimates. First column item, then one column per parameter.
adj_mat items x items matrix Item co-exposure counts (see construct_adj_mat()).

Two design choices are important to make clear:

  • Responses and simulation state are matrices. The full matrix of possible item responses, R, is unchanging throughout the simulation. The simulation state, admin, functions as a mask for this total response matrix. Items where admin == 0 have not yet been administered, and items where admin > 0 have. The positive values in admin additionally encode the order in which items were administered, which order-sensitive algorithms can use.
  • Parameters are data frames. This preserves the flexibility to carry arbitrary person or item parameters: add a column to pers or item and it flows through the simulation untouched while still interacting with existing selection and update functions.

If you need to work with long data frames inside your module, call meow_long(R, admin) to get the administered responses as an id/item/resp data frame, ordered by respondent and administration order. While this enables tidyverse-style operations, this will likely come at a significant efficiency cost.

R <- matrix(c(1, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2)
admin <- matrix(c(1L, 0L, 2L, 1L), nrow = 2)
meow_long(R, admin)
#>   id item resp
#> 1  1    1    1
#> 2  1    2    1
#> 3  2    2    1

Data loaders

A data loader sets up a simulation. It takes any arguments you like (passed through data_args) and returns a list with three elements:

  • resp: a long data frame with columns id, item, and resp.
  • pers_tru: true person parameters; first column id, then parameter names.
  • item_tru: true item parameters; first column item, then parameter names.

meow() turns resp into the response matrix R once, before the loop starts, so the loader’s only job is to produce the bank of potential responses and the ground-truth parameter values. Below, we implement a data loader that draws respondent abilities from two normal distributions. Note the call to set.seed(NULL) to clear the seed before item selection and parameter updates.

data_two_groups <- function(N_per_group = 50, N_items = 40, data_seed = 1) {
  set.seed(data_seed)
  N <- 2 * N_per_group
  theta <- c(stats::rnorm(N_per_group, -0.5), stats::rnorm(N_per_group, 0.5))
  b <- stats::rnorm(N_items)
  pers_tru <- data.frame(id = seq_len(N), theta = theta)
  item_tru <- data.frame(item = seq_len(N_items), b = b, a = 1)

  p <- stats::plogis(outer(theta, b, "-"))
  resp_mat <- matrix(stats::rbinom(length(p), 1, p), nrow = N)
  resp <- data.frame(
    id = rep(seq_len(N), each = N_items),
    item = rep(seq_len(N_items), times = N),
    resp = as.vector(t(resp_mat))
  )
  set.seed(NULL)
  list(resp = resp, pers_tru = pers_tru, item_tru = item_tru)
}

str(data_two_groups(N_per_group = 3, N_items = 4), max.level = 1)
#> List of 3
#>  $ resp    :'data.frame':    24 obs. of  3 variables:
#>  $ pers_tru:'data.frame':    6 obs. of  2 variables:
#>  $ item_tru:'data.frame':    4 obs. of  3 variables:

Reserved argument names a loader must not use are the ones the harness supplies to the other modules: pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, and select_seed.

Item selection functions

An item selection function decides which item to administer next. The standard signature is

select_fun <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, ...) { ... }

and it returns an administration matrix with the cells that correspond to newly chosen items marked non-zero. You only need to add items; the harness records the administration order for you, so setting a cell to TRUE or 1 is enough. Leave previously administered cells as they were. To stop administering items (a stopping rule), simply return admin unchanged — the simulation halts when an iteration adds nothing.

Here we present a complete custom selector that administers the easiest remaining item to each respondent, seeding the first five items in the bank on the first iteration:

select_easiest <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat = NULL) {
  if (!any(admin != 0)) {       # first iteration: seed five items
    admin[, seq_len(min(5, ncol(admin)))] <- 1L
    return(admin)
  }
  difficulty <- item$b
  for (i in which(rowSums(admin == 0) > 0)) {
    remaining <- which(admin[i, ] == 0)
    pick <- remaining[which.min(difficulty[remaining])]
    admin[i, pick] <- 1L
  }
  admin
}

A few tools we use that may be helpful to designing your own selection functions:

  • admin[i, ] == 0 gives the pool of unadministered items for respondent i.
  • rowSums(admin == 0) > 0 identifies respondents who still have items they have not yet responded to.
  • Setting admin[i, pick] <- 1L administers item pick to respondent i.

If you prefer to compute on long data, meow_long(R, admin) is always available (but discouraged).

Parameter update functions

A parameter update function re-estimates parameters from the administered responses. Its signature is

update_fun <- function(pers, item, R, admin, ...) { ... }

and it returns a list with two elements, pers and item, the updated data frames of parameter estimates. The administered responses are R[admin != 0], and their respondent and item indices can be found from which(admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE).

This custom updater nudges each ability toward the current proportion correct, without touching any item parameters:

update_pct_correct <- function(pers, item, R, admin, rate = 0.5) {
  idx <- which(admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE)
  person <- idx[, 1]
  resp <- R[idx]
  pct <- tapply(resp, person, mean)
  target <- stats::qlogis(pmin(pmax(pct, 0.02), 0.98)) # logit of proportion
  pers$theta[as.integer(names(target))] <-
    (1 - rate) * pers$theta[as.integer(names(target))] + rate * target
  list(pers = pers, item = item)
}

Putting it together

Custom modules plug into meow() exactly like the bundled ones. Extra arguments are passed through select_args, update_args, and data_args.

sim <- meow(
  select_fun  = select_easiest,
  update_fun  = update_pct_correct,
  data_loader = data_two_groups,
  data_args   = list(N_per_group = 25, N_items = 20),
  update_args = list(rate = 0.3),
  fix         = "item"
)

head(sim$results[, 1:4])
#>   iter pers_theta_1_est pers_theta_2_est pers_theta_3_est
#> 1    1        0.1216395       -0.1216395      -0.12163953
#> 2    2        0.2930918       -0.2930918      -0.08514767
#> 3    3        0.2914689       -0.4800515      -0.14590799
#> 4    4        0.3572759       -0.4892837      -0.25538328
#> 5    5        0.3170362       -0.4094417      -0.38671245
#> 6    6        0.2219253       -0.4082487      -0.39233825

The output object has the same shape no matter which modules you use: a results data frame (one row per iteration, an est and bias column for each parameter), a list of adjacency matrices in adj_mats, and the true parameters in pers_tru and item_tru. That consistency is what lets you reuse analysis and plotting code across studies; see vignette("intro-viz").

Checklist

  • Data loaders return list(resp, pers_tru, item_tru) with long resp.
  • Item selection functions take (pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, ...) and return an administration matrix with new cells marked non-zero.
  • Parameter update functions take (pers, item, R, admin, ...) and return list(pers, item).
  • Use admin != 0 (or meow_administered()) for the administered mask, and meow_long() if you want long data frames.
  • Do not attempt to un-administer items.
  • Implement stopping rules by declining to administer additional items for completed individuals. Once admin doesn’t change between iterations, the simulation will terminate.

On this page

Developed by Klint Kanopka, Sophia Deng.
